params.text:
Sepsis awareness and reporting

Sepsis - act fast

Sepsis is the body's extreme, life-threatening response to an infection. It can develop within hours and is a medical emergency - the sooner it is treated, the better the chance of survival. You are often the first person to notice a change. Escalate urgently if someone shows signs such as:

  • Slurred speech, new confusion or unusual drowsiness.
  • Extreme shivering, or muscle pain.
  • Passing little or no urine.
  • Severe breathlessness or fast breathing.
  • Skin that is mottled, blotchy, very pale or bluish.
  • A rash that does not fade when you press a glass against it.

If you suspect sepsis, call 999 or get emergency help immediately and say "I am worried about sepsis." Do not wait to "see how they go."

Reporting and notifiable diseases

Report infection concerns promptly so action can be taken:

  • Tell your manager and follow your provider's outbreak plan if two or more people have similar symptoms (e.g. diarrhoea and vomiting).
  • Certain notifiable diseases (such as measles, tuberculosis, food poisoning and others) must be reported to UKHSA - usually via a registered clinician or your local health protection team.
  • Work-related exposures and some infections may be reportable under RIDDOR. Record sharps injuries and exposures.
  • Keep good records - accurate reporting protects the people you support and helps stop outbreaks early.

params.question: You visit Mrs Okafor, who had a mild urine infection. She is now suddenly confused, shivering, breathing fast and her skin looks mottled. What should you do?

answers.text: Treat it as a possible sepsis emergency - call 999 straight away and say you are worried about sepsis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. These are red-flag signs of sepsis. Sepsis can be fatal within hours, so seek emergency help immediately.

answers.text: Make her comfortable and note it in the care record to mention at the next visit

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No - this delay could be fatal. These signs need emergency assessment now.

answers.text: Give her paracetamol and wait an hour to see if she improves

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No - waiting is dangerous with suspected sepsis. Every hour matters; call 999 immediately.

answers.text: Phone the GP surgery and leave a message for a call back

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Too slow for a suspected emergency. Suspected sepsis needs 999, not a routine GP message.

params.text:
Key takeaways

You have covered the essentials of infection prevention and control for frontline care:

  • Break the chain of infection - your everyday actions target its links.
  • Apply standard precautions to everyone, every time.
  • Hand hygiene at the WHO 5 Moments is your most powerful tool - soap and water for C. difficile and norovirus.
  • Use PPE by task; remember donning (apron, mask, eyes, gloves) and doffing (gloves, apron, eyes, mask).
  • Handle sharps, waste and spillages safely, and keep the environment clean.
  • Recognise HCAIs, act fast on sepsis, and report and record promptly.

Thank you for helping keep the people you support safe.
